did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet121, and ResNet50 with transfer learning, to classify clinical images of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK). The fine-tuned InceptionResNetV2 model achieved an accuracy of 0.855 and an AUC of 0.919, and its performance was compared against 21 dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' and presents original experimental work, including the construction of a new Chinese skin disease dataset (1456 BCC and 1843 SK images), the development and evaluation of CNN models (InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet121, ResNet50), and a comparative study against 21 dermatologists.

title: Assistant Diagnosis of Basal Cell Carcinoma and Seborrheic Keratosis in Chinese Population Using Convolutional Neural Network
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in distinguishing clinical images of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK) in a Chinese population. The researchers constructed a novel dataset comprising 1,456 BCC and 1,843 SK clinical images with pathological verification. Four mainstream CNN architectures (InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet121, and ResNet50) were fine-tuned using transfer learning for binary classification. The fine-tuned InceptionResNetV2 model achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 0.855 and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.919. The model's diagnostic capability was compared against 21 dermatologists on an additional test set of 116 images, demonstrating performance comparable to expert dermatologists. The study concludes that CNN models offer a promising, interpretable assistant diagnosis tool for BCC and SK.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin tumor; 1456 images in the constructed dataset; binary classification target.
Benign skin tumor; 1843 images in the constructed dataset; binary classification target.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly builds a 'binary classifier' to distinguish between two discrete diagnostic categories: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK). The models (InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet121, ResNet50) were fine-tuned for this binary classification task, with performance evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC. Dermatologists also provided binary answers (BCC or SK).

datasets_used.name: Chinese Skin Disease Dataset (Xiangya Hospital)
datasets_used.description: A novel dataset constructed from clinical images collected at Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, between January 2016 and December 2018. Images were obtained using a high-definition digital camera (SONY DSC-HX50), annotated by 12 dermatologists, cropped to 300x300 resolution, and verified by pathological examination. The dataset includes corresponding medical history.

use.training: Used to train and fine-tune four CNN architectures (InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet121, ResNet50) using transfer learning and from scratch.
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance during the training process (verification portion).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the CNN models (accuracy, AUC, etc.).
use.other: The dataset labels and whole dataset were made publicly available for academic research.

datasets_used.name: Additional Test Set
datasets_used.description: A separate set of clinical images collected from January 2019 to June 2019, used specifically for comparing the CNN model's performance against dermatologists.

use.testing: Used to compare the diagnostic performance of the fine-tuned InceptionResNetV2 model against 21 dermatologists (8 expert and 13 general).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the CNN model (InceptionResNetV2) against 21 dermatologists (8 expert and 13 general) on an additional test set of 116 images, specifically calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC) and True Positive Rate (TPR)/False Positive Rate (FPR) at different thresholds.
To visualize the high-dimensional features extracted by the CNN model (reducing 1536-dimensional features to 2D or 3D) for interpretability and to show the separation between BCC and SK classes.
To generate saliency maps highlighting essential regions of the input image important for the prediction category, enhancing model interpretability.
To generate corresponding saliency maps for the input image to view the classification basis of the CNN diagnostic model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons between the CNN model and dermatologists were made using descriptive statistics such as Accuracy, AUC, True Positive Rate (TPR), and False Positive Rate (FPR), with qualitative statements like 'comparable to' or 'superior to' rather than statistical significance testing.

Chinese Skin Disease Dataset (Test Set).description: Test set from the constructed dataset (91 BCC, 152 SK images) used to evaluate CNN models trained from scratch vs fine-tuned.
Chinese Skin Disease Dataset (Test Set).notes: Fine-tuned models outperformed those trained from scratch in AUC. InceptionResNetV2 (fine-tuned) achieved the highest AUC (0.919) and Accuracy (0.855).

Additional Test Set (CNN vs Dermatologis.description: 116 clinical images collected from Jan 2019 to Jun 2019, used to compare the fine-tuned InceptionResNetV2 model against 21 dermatologists (8 expert, 13 general).
Additional Test Set (CNN vs Dermatologis.notes: The CNN model's performance (AUC 0.937) was comparable to the average of expert dermatologists and superior to the average of general dermatologists. The best dermatologist achieved a TPR of 0.897 and FPR of 0.

architecture_used.main_model: Four mainstream CNN architectures: InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, DenseNet121, and ResNet50. The fine-tuned InceptionResNetV2 achieved the best performance.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained weights on ImageNet were used for the fine-tuning approach. Models were also trained from scratch for comparison.
architecture_used.input_size: 300 x 300 pixels (cropped and resized clinical images).

architecture_details.modification: The output dimension of the last fully connected layer was set to 2 for binary classification.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: InceptionResNetV2 extracted 1536-dimensional features before the final classification layer.

training_details.hardware: Three Nvidia Titan XP graphic processing units

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Seborrheic Keratosis)

preprocessing_techniques: Annotation of skin lesion location by 12 dermatologists using LabelImg software
Filtering of low-quality images (blurred images, lesions covered with hair or other visual elements, lesions altered by topical treatments)
Cropping of images based on annotation files to isolate skin lesions
Resizing of cropped images to 300x300 resolution
conclusions: The study is the first to propose an assistant diagnosis system for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK) based on a Chinese population dataset.
The fine-tuned InceptionResNetV2 model achieved the best classification performance among the tested CNN architectures, with an accuracy of 0.855 and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.919.
Fine-tuned CNN models outperformed models trained from scratch in terms of AUC.
The CNN model's diagnostic performance was comparable to that of expert dermatologists and superior to the average of general dermatologists on an additional test set.
The CNN model demonstrated interpretability through t-SNE, Grad-CAM, and LIME, showing that it focuses on relevant lesion regions.
The model has potential applications in assisting dermatologists in remote communities and recommending biopsy locations.

Limitations include the relatively small dataset size, which requires more samples to verify generalization, and the lack of patient metadata (e.g., age, gender) which could improve diagnostic ability through multimodal input.
The feasibility of the proposed biopsy location recommendation application needs to be verified by clinical trials.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a relatively small dataset size (3,299 images), which may affect the generalization performance of the model.

The model relies solely on clinical images as input, ignoring patient metadata (age, gender, lesion location) and medical history, which limits its clinical utility compared to dermatologists who use multimodal information.
The comparison with dermatologists was conducted on a small additional test set of only 116 images, which may limit the statistical power of the performance comparison.
The proposed application of recommending biopsy locations via LIME interpretability maps is tentative and lacks verification through clinical trials.
The dataset is specific to a Chinese population from Xiangya Hospital, potentially introducing population bias and limiting immediate generalizability to other ethnic groups.

The study did not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the CNN model and dermatologists, relying instead on descriptive metrics like AUC and TPR/FPR.

The best-performing dermatologist achieved a True Positive Rate of 0.897 and False Positive Rate of 0, indicating that while the CNN model was comparable to the *average* expert dermatologist, it did not surpass the best individual expert.
